overview.title: Respiratory Illnesses in New York City
overview.subtitle:
This webpage summarizes data on three respiratory viruses - COVID-19, flu (influenza), and RSV (respiratory syncytial virus).

The Health Department uses information from a variety of sources to capture a comprehensive picture of respiratory illness activity citywide.
overview.updateNote: This page is updated every Thursday with data through the previous Saturday.
overview.edDisclaimer: Data from EDs often lack information on demographic characteristics like race and ethnicity. We know this lack of representation in data is inequitable, and are working to ensure more inclusive, equitable representation in the future.
overview.disclaimer: All data are preliminary and may change as we receive more information. Some data on these pages lack information on demographic characteristics like race and ethnicity. The NYC Health Department acknowledges this lack of representation in the data is inequitable, and we are committed to developing methods for ensuring more inclusive, equitable representation in the future. Learn more about health inequities in data.

statGrid.title: What's happening across the city?
statGrid.subtitle: Emergency Department trends for the week ending

monthlyARIChart.title: Percent of {viewLabel} {viewLabelPreposition} the Emergency Department that have a Respiratory Illness Diagnosis
monthlyARIChart.staticSubtitle: These data show trends of people who received a respiratory illness diagnosis in an NYC emergency department and do not represent everyone with respiratory illness. For example, people who received care outside of an emergency department or who never sought care for their illness are not included.

ari.title: Overall Respiratory Illness
ari.infoText: COVID-19, flu, RSV, and other diagnoses

summaryBox.title: Emergency Department Trends for the Week Ending {date}
summaryBox.description:
We examine data on visits to the emergency department (ED visits) that result in a diagnosis of COVID-19, the flu, or RSV - as well as general respiratory complaints like cough or pneumonia.

Data on ED visits provide a timely view of changes in respiratory virus spread. But, they don't capture people who are sick but don't seek care, or get care somewhere other than an emergency department.

otherResp.title: Respiratory Panel Results
otherResp.intro: The NYC Health Department receives data from selected NYC laboratories that test for an expanded panel of circulating respiratory viruses, including those that are not required to be reported to the NYC Health Department.
otherResp.sentence: For the week of {date}, the percent of positive test results for other respiratory viruses were: {list}.

emergencyDeptPage.mainSubtitle: Respiratory virus season usually begins in the fall and ends in the spring, but not all respiratory infections follow a seasonal pattern. Flu and RSV are seasonal and most prevalent during the fall and winter months. To date, COVID-19 has generally increased during the fall and winter months as well as other times during the year, including the summer.

noRaceEthnicitySection.title: Information about Race and Ethnicity
noRaceEthnicitySection.body: Data received from emergency departments often lack information on demographic characteristics like race and ethnicity. The NYC Health Department acknowledges this lack of representation in the data is inequitable, and we are committed to developing methods for ensuring more inclusive, equitable representation in the future.

caseDataPage.mainSubtitle: Respiratory virus season usually begins in the fall and ends in the spring, but not all respiratory infections follow a seasonal pattern. Flu and RSV are seasonal and most prevalent during the fall and winter months. To date, COVID-19 has generally increased during the fall and winter months as well as other times during the year, including the summer.

noRaceEthnicitySection.title: Information about Race and Ethnicity
noRaceEthnicitySection.body: Flu and RSV data received from electronic laboratory reports often lack information on demographic characteristics, such as race and ethnicity. The NYC Health Department acknowledges this lack of representation in the data is inequitable, and we are committed to developing methods for ensuring more inclusive, equitable representation in the future.

aboutPage.mainSubtitle:
The Respiratory Illness Data Pages show data on COVID-19, flu (influenza), and RSV (respiratory syncytial virus) in New York City collected by the NYC Health Department.

We want to continually improve this website to make it as useful as possible for you. Take our quick survey to let us know what is working and how we can improve.
